The original Pacman ghost AI is iconic precisely BECAUSE the four ghosts have different personalities — red enemy (chase), pink enemy (ambush), Inky (vector), orange enemy (patrol). Players unconsciously learn to read each ghost's pattern and play around them. Uniform random picks make the gameplay 'noisy random' rather than 'pattern-based.' Generalizes to any pursuit game on a grid — dungeon crawlers, hide-and-seek, top-down chase games. Different from arcade-action's jitter rule (game-bot-ai-jitter-not-laser-accurate), which applies to free-2D AI with continuous aim.
examples: RIGHT (Pacman): enum Personality { case chaser, ambusher, patrol, random }; on each enemy tick, pickEnemyDirection() switches on personality. red enemy = chaser, pink enemy = ambusher, orange enemy = patrol, Sue = random.
RIGHT (ambusher target projection): `let target = projectedTarget(from: hero.position, direction: hero.direction, distance: 4)`; pick walkable that minimizes Manhattan to target.
RIGHT (frightened override): all personalities flip to maximize-distance-from-hero when actor.frightenedTicks > 0.
WRONG: all ghosts use the same `pickGhostDirection()` random walkable. They feel like a swarm — players don't develop the 'oh, the orange one is dumb so I can run past it' intuition.

guidelines.rule: Tower-defense / wave-based games SHOULD inject a DIFFERENT enemy mix on boss waves, not just 'same composition + 1 boss enemy.' Pattern: when `LevelManager.isBossLevel` is true, call `engine.startWave(composition:)` with a heavy-tank-dominated mix (e.g. 5 normal + 6 heavy + 2 fast) instead of the default `startWave(count:)` that uses the engine's progressive default. Heavy-dominated boss waves require fundamentally different tower placement strategy (more cannons / fewer fast-tracking) — that's what makes the boss wave feel special vs 'wave 5 has 1 extra enemy.' Pair with a distinct entry SFX (arcadePowerUp) and a 'BOSS WAVE' label in status text. Without composition injection, the boss-wave concept reduces to a number on a screen.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with TowerDefense v2 polish. TowerDefenseEngine already had `startWave(composition: [(EnemyKind, Int)])` as a public API but no example used it for boss-flavored waves. The View now invokes the composition variant on every 5th wave. Generalizes to any wave-based game with multiple enemy types — lane-defense (lots of conehead zombies on round 5), Bloons (more lead bloons on the round-5 boss), Kingdom Rush, etc.
examples: RIGHT: if levels.isBossLevel { engine.startWave(composition: [(.normal, 5), (.heavy, 6), (.fast, 2)]) } else { engine.startWave(count: 7 + engine.wave) }
RIGHT (visual): boss-wave status text reads 'BOSS WAVE 5 — heavy assault incoming' and the entry SFX is arcadePowerUp instead of uiButtonTap.
WRONG: boss wave = same composition + 1 extra enemy. Players feel no strategic difference.

guidelines.rule: When wiring SFX to a game-engine tick (vector-shooter arcade/paddle-bounce/Pacman/lane-hopper arcade/TowerDefense pattern), use the pre/post snapshot model: capture `preLives = engine.lives` / `preScore = engine.score` / `preEnemyCount = engine.enemies.count` BEFORE calling `engine.update()`, then compare against post-update values. Diff > 0 = an event occurred this tick → fire the appropriate SFX. This is the View-side complement to the engine `lastEvent` / `consumeLastEvent()` pattern (game-engine-event-consumer-pattern) — pre/post snapshot is for engines that don't expose discrete events but DO have observable scalar state (score, lives, enemy count). Critical: do this BEFORE `if engine.phase == .playing` checks since some events (game-over, win) are themselves the phase change.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 from Pacman/lane-hopper arcade/TD v2 wiring. Earlier ContentView code had `engine.update()` followed by status text mutation, with no SFX hook. The pre/post snapshot pattern surfaces all discrete events the engine produces without requiring an `Event` enum. Tower kills + base breaches in TD: postEnemyCount < preEnemyCount tells you something disappeared; livesLost = preLives - postLives. Tower kills = (enemies that disappeared) - (enemies that reached the base). Composable, debounce-friendly, and works across any tick-based game.
examples: RIGHT: let preLives = engine.lives; let preEnemyCount = engine.enemies.count; engine.update(dt: dt); let livesLost = preLives - engine.lives; let killedThisTick = preEnemyCount - engine.enemies.count; let towerKills = max(0, killedThisTick - livesLost); if towerKills > 0 { sound.play(.arcadeExplosion) }; if livesLost > 0 { sound.play(.uiError) }

RIGHT (Pacman): scoreDelta = engine.score - scoreBefore; if scoreDelta >= 200 { sound.play(.match3Burst) /* ghost eaten */ } else if scoreDelta >= 100 { sound.play(.victory, pitchSemitones: 4) /* fruit */ } else if scoreDelta >= 50 { sound.play(.arcadePowerUp) /* power pellet */ } else if scoreDelta >= 10 { sound.play(.match3Pop) /* pellet */ }
WRONG: trying to detect events by polling state every render frame — you can't tell *which tick* a delta happened on, debounce becomes impossible.

guidelines.rule: Reference apps that share a game engine (e.g. Pacman + lane-hopper arcade both using `GridAdventureEngine`) MUST configure the engine via STATIC FACTORY METHODS that return distinct configurations, NOT by hardcoded conditionals inside the engine. Pattern: `static func froggerLaneMap() -> Engine` and `static func pacmanArcadeMap() -> Engine` — each returns a fully-configured engine with the appropriate map, actor roster, and per-actor properties (e.g. ghost personalities). The engine itself stays game-agnostic — it doesn't know whether it's Pacman or lane-hopper arcade. View-level differences (cell size, color palette, status text) live in the View. This separation lets the same engine power 5+ reference apps with different feels without engine bloat.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with Pacman/lane-hopper arcade v2. GridAdventureEngine has factory methods for adventure-castle / frogger-lane / maze-pellet / pacman-arcade maps. Each returns a distinct engine state. Without this pattern, a single ContentView would have to construct + populate the engine inline (200+ lines of map data per game) and any engine API changes would break all factories silently. With factory methods, the engine evolves cleanly and new games are just new factories.
examples: RIGHT: GridAdventureEngine.pacmanArcadeMap() returns engine with maze + 4 ghosts (each with personality), pellets, fruit, power pellets. froggerLaneMap() returns same engine type with road/water tiles + vehicle/log actors.
WRONG: GridAdventureEngine has `var gameMode: GameMode` field and switches behavior on it. Engine becomes a god object that handles every game type.

guidelines.rule: To detect whether a five-letter-guess puzzle-style guess row is fully filled, use `row.every(c => c)` (all cells truthy) or `row.some(c => !c)` (any cell empty). NEVER use `row.includes("")` or `guess.includes("")` — both `String.prototype.includes("")` and `Array.prototype.includes("")` ALWAYS return true (the empty string/element is considered present in every sequence), so the check fires 'Fill the full row first' even when the row is fully filled. Same trap for `"".indexOf("") === 0` and similar empty-arg patterns.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-13: AIWizard word-quest v27 emitted `if (guess.length !== WORD_LEN || guess.includes(""))` for row-validation. `"abcde".includes("")` returns true, so the OR is always-true and the toast 'Fill the full row first' fires on every Enter, blocking submission even with a complete row. JS spec: ECMA-262 §22.1.3.10 / §23.1.3.16 — searchString '' is found at index 0 of any string/array.
examples: RIGHT (array of cell chars): if (row.length !== WORD_LEN || row.some(c => !c)) { toast('Fill the full row first'); return; }
RIGHT (array, positive form): if (row.length === WORD_LEN && row.every(c => c)) { submitGuess(row.join('')); }
RIGHT (joined string with placeholder '_' for empty): if (guessStr.length !== WORD_LEN || guessStr.includes('_')) { ... } — only correct when EMPTY cells render as a real character
WRONG: if (guess.length !== WORD_LEN || guess.includes('')) — second clause is always true
WRONG: if (guess.indexOf('') === -1) — indexOf('') always returns 0, never -1

guidelines.rule: Crossword grids MUST be word-valid in BOTH directions. Every contiguous letter sequence of length >=2 between black cells (or grid edges) — across AND down — MUST be a real English word verified against `window.mchatai.dictionary('english')` (or `english-3letter`/`english-4letter`/`english-5letter` for length-specific checks) before the puzzle is rendered. Validation must run on the GRID itself, not just on the cells the clues point at — a clue 'Vision organs' with intended answer EYES (4 letters) placed in a 5-cell slot, padded to EVYES to fit, is a critical failure. The clue's length MUST match the slot length AND every cross-word formed by intersecting letters MUST also be valid. If a slot can't be filled with a real word that satisfies every intersection, regenerate the puzzle (different theme, different grid pattern) rather than ship invented gibberish. Apply equally to LLM-generated puzzles, procedural puzzles, and hand-curated puzzle banks — same validation pass before any puzzle is shown to the user.
guidelines.why: Reported 2026-05-27 (Mini Crossword v3 in production): 3-Down clue 'Vision organs' rendered as EVYES (not a word) because the LLM placed EYES into a 5-cell slot without validating the resulting grid sequences. 5-Across read STLES (also gibberish, clue was 'Manners'). Players who tried to solve hit unsolvable cells and lost trust in the puzzle. pg-006 mentions 'all clues must have correct answers in the grid' but didn't make explicit that EVERY cross-direction sequence must also be a valid word — that gap is what shipped EVYES/STLES. The runtime API window.mchatai.dictionary (docs/MCHATAI_FUNDAMENTALS.md) makes this trivially enforceable — one .contains() call per across+down slot before render.
examples: RIGHT (validate before render): const dict = window.mchatai.dictionary('english'); const slots = collectSlots(grid); for (const slot of slots) { if (!dict.contains(slot.letters.toLowerCase())) { throw new Error(`Invalid grid: ${slot.direction} at (${slot.row},${slot.col}) = ${slot.letters} not in dictionary`); } }

RIGHT (LLM prompt anchor): 'Before emitting the puzzle JSON, mentally trace every across AND down sequence between black cells; reject the grid if ANY sequence is not a real English word. Do NOT pad short answers with random letters to fit longer slots — change the grid pattern instead.'

RIGHT (clue↔slot length check): assert(clue.answer.length === slot.length, `Clue ${n} answer '${clue.answer}' (${clue.answer.length}ch) does not fit ${slot.direction} slot at (${slot.row},${slot.col}) which is ${slot.length}ch`)
WRONG: place EYES (4 letters) into a 5-cell down slot by padding with a random letter → EVYES (not a word, breaks the cross-word at every intersection)
WRONG: validate only the across answers and skip down (or vice versa) — both directions form words and both must be valid
WRONG: ship a fallback puzzle on validation failure that itself wasn't validated — the fallback bank MUST pass the same checks

guidelines.rule: Daily/replayable puzzle games SHOULD aim for a bank of >=100 unique puzzles (was: >=10 in pg-bank-rule; raised 2026-05-27 per user feedback 'please have at least 100 valid crosswords'). For LLM-generated puzzles this means the recipe must include a 'generate-bank' phase that produces 100+ puzzles AT GENERATION TIME, validates each via pg-016, persists the validated bank to local storage, and rotates by day-of-year. For hand-curated banks (MiniCrosswordBank.swift et al.) treat <=10 as v1 catalog only — file a followup to grow before promoting to user-facing recipe lists. Showing 'Puzzle 3 of 100' is a quality signal; 'Puzzle 3 of 5' tells the user the bank is thin.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-27 on the catalog Mini Crossword: 'I should be able to go to the next crossword. Please have at least 100 valid crosswords!'. Prior bank rule (pg-bank-rule) set the floor at 10 — that was a v1 minimum but doesn't satisfy users who play daily and burn through the bank in a fortnight. Generating 100 at install time (with strict pg-016 validation per puzzle) is a one-time cost that pays off for the life of the install.
examples: RIGHT (LLM-generated bank): on first launch, run 100 generate+validate loops; each loop calls the cloud LLM to propose a grid, then runs pg-016 dictionary checks; if a grid fails, regenerate; persist the 100 valid puzzles to IndexedDB keyed by recipe + grid hash.
RIGHT (curated bank growth): MiniCrosswordBank.swift starts at 10, scheduled growth to 100 tracked as an explicit followup ticket. Show 'Puzzle X of N' in UI so the user sees current bank size.
WRONG: ship 5 hand-curated puzzles and call it done — users hit the end of the bank in a week and stop playing.
WRONG: generate puzzles on-the-fly with no validation cache — every load risks an invalid grid (pg-016) AND wastes tokens regenerating the same content.
